title: Google Spanner & TrueTime — Global Strong Consistency at Planetary Scale
subtitle: How Google's globally distributed database achieves serializable transactions across continents using atomic clocks and GPS — and what it means for SAP's multi-region future

sections.content:
## The Problem Spanner Solves

Before Spanner, distributed databases faced a fundamental tradeoff: you could have **strong consistency** (CockroachDB, TiDB) with limited geographic distribution, or **global scale** (DynamoDB, Cassandra) with only eventual consistency. You couldn't have both.

Spanner breaks this tradeoff using **TrueTime** — a globally synchronized clock infrastructure based on GPS receivers and atomic clocks. With TrueTime, Spanner provides **external consistency** (linearizability at global scale) with **read-only access to all data from any replica** and **strongly consistent global transactions**.

For SAP architects: Spanner's architecture directly informs SAP HANA Cloud's multi-region strategy. If SAP ever needs globally consistent transactions across continents (think: global SAP S/4HANA deployments with cross-region financial consolidation), Spanner's design is the blueprint.

### What Makes Spanner Unique

| Feature | Spanner | Traditional NewSQL | Legacy RDBMS (incl. HANA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Consistency model | External (linearizable) globally | Linearizable within region | Strong within single DB |
| Geographic scale | Planetary (any number of regions) | Single or few regions | Single datacenter |
| Replication | Automatic, configurable per table | Manual or replica sets | Manual (HANA SR) |
| Clock sync | TrueTime (GPS + atomic clocks) | NTP (millisecond accuracy) | NTP |
| Read scalability | Fresnel reads (lock-free, consistent) | Follower reads (stale) | Read-only replicas (stale) |
| Schema | Relational with SQL | Relational | Relational

cards.title: TrueTime — The Foundation
cards.body:
TrueTime exposes a single API: `TT.interval()` returns `[earliest, latest]` — the current time as an interval of uncertainty. The true absolute time is guaranteed to fall within this interval.

**Hardware:** Each Spanner datacenter has a **time master** per machine: one GPS receiver with a dedicated antenna, plus one atomic clock (Arm embedded). GPS provides ~1ms accuracy; atomic clocks provide backup during GPS outages.

**Uncertainty:** Typical TT.now() interval is 1-7ms. This uncertainty bounds how long Spanner must wait before committing a write.

**No NTP!** Google explicitly avoids NTP because its millisecond-level synchronization is insufficient for the microsecond-precision commit ordering Spanner needs.

cards.title: Paxos for Replication
cards.body:
Each Spanner tablet (a partition of rows) is replicated across zones using **Paxos**. The Paxos leader lives in one zone; all writes go through the leader.

**Lease mechanism:** The leader holds a timestamp-based lease from its replicas. The lease ensures that if the leader fails, no other node can accept writes for a known interval — preventing split-brain.

**Writes:** The leader assigns a commit timestamp (using TrueTime), replicates via Paxos, and commits. The client sees commit timestamp T. Future reads >= T are guaranteed to see this write.

cards.title: Lock-Free Consistent Reads (Fresnel)
cards.body:
Spanner's most elegant trick: **read-only transactions don't need locks**. A read-only transaction picks a timestamp `t` (usually the last Paxos write timestamp) and executes against any replica that is sufficiently up-to-date.

If a replica hasn't applied all writes <= `t`, the read waits (usually sub-millisecond). No locking, no blocking writers.

**Result:** Reads scale linearly with the number of replicas. A 100-replica Spanner deployment can handle 100x the read throughput of a single-node database — while maintaining strong consistency.

cards.title: TrueTime + Paxos = External Consistency
cards.body:
The key insight: Spanner guarantees **external consistency** (aka linearizability or one-copy serializability) by using TrueTime to order commits.

Transaction A commits at TrueTime [a1, a2]. Transaction B starts after A commits — so B's start time is >= a2. When B tries to commit at [b1, b2], Spanner assigns it timestamp b1. Since b1 >= a2, the timestamps respect real-time ordering.

**The Commit Wait:** The leader must wait until `TT.after(t)` is true (i.e., `t < TT.now().earliest`). This wait is equal to the uncertainty interval — typically 1-7ms. Without this wait, a later transaction could receive a timestamp that violates external consistency.

sections.content:
## Spanner Architecture — Universe, Zones, and Tablets

### Universe → Zone → Tablet

A **Spanner universe** is the entire deployment (e.g., production). Each universe is divided into **zones**, which are roughly one datacenter. Each zone contains:

- **One zonemaster** — assigns tablets to spanservers
- **One spanserver per 100-1000 tablets** — serves data to clients
- **One location proxy** — routes clients to the closest replica

A **tablet** is a data structure that holds a contiguous range of rows (similar to HANA's partition). Tablets are replicated via Paxos across zones. The replication configuration is **per-table** — a table can be in 1 zone (low-latency, low-availability) or 5 zones (high-latency, high-availability).

### The Directory Abstraction

Spanner groups related rows into **directories** (not to be confused with filesystem directories). A directory is the unit of:

- **Data movement** — directories are moved between tablets for load balancing
- **Replication** — all rows in a directory share the same replication config
- **Atomic operations** — transactions within a single directory use a single Paxos group (fast); transactions across directories use two-phase commit (slower)

**Directory-aware client placement:** The client can specify which rows should be in the same directory — even rows from different tables (e.g., User table and Order table for the same user_id). This enables single-Paxos-group transactions for the most common access patterns.

sections.title: Read vs Write Protocol
rows: Pick timestamp t <= last committed. Execute at any replica with all writes <= t applied.
Same as single-key read, but executed on multiple tablets. No locks needed.
Leader acquires TrueTime interval, assigns commit ts, replicates via Paxos, waits for TT.after(commit_ts).
Participant leaders each prepare. Coordinator picks commit ts, replicates prepare + commit records. All participants wait TT.after(ts).
Pick timestamp t that may be in the past. Execute at any replica regardless of applied writes.

sections.title: Spanner DDL — Interleaved Tables (Directory Groups)
sections.code:
-- Spanner SQL for creating interleaved parent-child tables.
-- Rows with the same SingerId are co-located in the same directory,
-- enabling single-Paxos-group transactions for the common access pattern.

CREATE TABLE Singers (
  SingerId   INT64 NOT NULL,
  FirstName  STRING(1024),
  LastName   STRING(1024),
  SingerInfo BYTES(MAX),
) PRIMARY KEY (SingerId);

CREATE TABLE Albums (
  SingerId     INT64 NOT NULL,
  AlbumId      INT64 NOT NULL,
  AlbumTitle   STRING(MAX),
) PRIMARY KEY (SingerId, AlbumId),
  INTERLEAVE IN PARENT Singers ON DELETE CASCADE;

-- All albums for a singer are co-located with the singer row.
-- A transaction updating a Singer and inserting Albums in one
-- atomic operation uses a single Paxos group (fast path).

sections.content:
## SAP Bridge — What Spanner Means for SAP HANA Cloud

### Current SAP HANA Cloud Multi-Region Architecture

SAP HANA Cloud currently offers **cross-region replication via HANA System Replication (HSR)**:
- Primary in region 1 (e.g., US East)
- Secondary in region 2 (e.g., EU West)
- Failover is manual or scripted, not transparent to applications
- Reads from secondary may return stale data
- Cross-region transactions require application-level coordination (XA transactions or SAGA patterns)

### What Spanner-Style Architecture Would Enable

If SAP were to build a Spanner-inspired global HANA:

| Capability | Today (HANA SR) | Spanner-Inspired |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-region reads | Stale (async replication) | Strongly consistent (TrueTime + Paxos) |
| Cross-region writes | Manual failover only | Transparent, any region |
| Transaction consistency | Per-region (XA for cross-region) | Global external consistency |
| Replication lag | Seconds to minutes | ~100ms (Paxos commit) |
| Failover time | Minutes (manual) | < 10s (automatic leader election) |
| Schema changes | Manual per-region | Global DDL via Paxos

### The Key Gap

SAP HANA would need a clock synchronization infrastructure comparable to TrueTime. Without atomic clocks + GPS, the clock uncertainty is orders of magnitude larger (NTP: 1-50ms vs TrueTime: 1-7ms). Large clock uncertainty means longer commit-wait times, which increases write latency.

**Practical takeaway:** For SAP architects interviewing at FAANG, understanding TrueTime's role in enabling global external consistency is the single most important distributed systems insight you can demonstrate. It's the key differentiator between 'we use Paxos/Raft for replication' and 'we build globally consistent databases.'

sections.title: Spanner & TrueTime FAQ for Staff+ Interviews

tabs.label: TrueTime
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How does TrueTime enable external consistency?"

**Staff+ answer:** "TrueTime exposes time as an interval `[earliest, latest]` where the true time is guaranteed to be somewhere in between. The key insight is **commit-wait**: when a transaction commits, the leader assigns a timestamp `t` and waits until `TT.after(t)` is true — meaning `t < TT.now().earliest`. This wait equals the clock uncertainty (1-7ms).

Now consider two transactions A and B where B starts after A committed. Since A committed before B's start time, A's commit timestamp `t_a < TT.now().earliest_b_start`. When B picks `t_b = TT.now().latest` as its timestamp, we have `t_a < t_b`. The commit timestamps respect real-time ordering — this is external consistency."

**Why this impresses:** You explained not just the mechanism but the *proof* of why it guarantees external consistency. The inequality chain is elegant and shows genuine depth.

tabs.label: Comparison
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How is Spanner different from CockroachDB?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Both use Paxos/Raft for replication and support distributed SQL. The **fundamental difference** is clock infrastructure.

CockroachDB uses **hybrid logical clocks (HLC)** — a software-only approach that tracks physical time and logical counters. HLC guarantees causal consistency but not external consistency because physical clock skew is unbounded.

Spanner uses **TrueTime** — hardware-backed (GPS + atomic clocks) with bounded uncertainty (1-7ms). This enables the commit-wait mechanism that guarantees external consistency.

**Practical impact:** CockroachDB can serialize 'causally related' writes but may reorder 'concurrent' writes in ways that violate external consistency. Spanner never does. However, CockroachDB is simpler to deploy (no special hardware).

**Meta-point:** The tradeoff is operational complexity vs consistency guarantees. Spanner is harder to run but provides guarantees that CockroachDB cannot match. CockroachDB is easier to run but provides slightly weaker guarantees."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a precise, technical comparison with a clear meta-point about tradeoffs. You know both systems intimately.

tabs.label: SAP Angle
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Could SAP build a Spanner?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Theoretically yes, but with significant challenges.

**What SAP already has:** SAP HANA Cloud runs on AWS/Azure/GCP. Google deploys Spanner on its own infrastructure with custom time masters per datacenter. SAP could deploy TrueTime-like infrastructure on cloud VMs using **Amazon Time Sync Service** (microsecond accuracy within an AZ) or **Azure Precision Time Protocol (PTP)**.

**The hard part:** Cross-cloud clock sync. If HANA Cloud runs across AWS and GCP, the clock uncertainty jumps to 10-100ms (NTP across cloud providers). Commit-wait at 100ms makes write latency impractical for transactional workloads.

**Practical path:** Start with single-cloud multi-region (all HANA Cloud within AWS, using AWS Time Sync). This keeps clock uncertainty to ~1ms — comparable to TrueTime. True cross-cloud Spanner would require inventing a multi-cloud TrueTime equivalent."

**Why this impresses:** You showed practical knowledge of cloud clock services (Amazon Time Sync, Azure PTP), understood the cross-cloud limitation, and proposed a concrete migration path. This is the difference between a theorist and an engineer.

tabs.label: Design Choice
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Why does Spanner use Paxos and not Raft?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Two reasons:

1. **Paxos predates Raft** — Spanner was designed in 2011-2012, when Raft didn't exist (Raft was published 2014). Google had extensive Paxos experience from Chubby and Bigtable.

2. **Paxos enables Flexible Paxos / Multi-Paxos** — Spanner uses an optimized variant where read operations can bypass the leader if the quorum has seen recent writes. Raft's leader-centric model doesn't support this as naturally.

That said, the difference is **mostly academic** for Spanner's use case. Both provide the same consistency guarantees. Raft is simpler to implement; Paxos is more flexible. If Spanner were built today, they'd consider Raft but likely still choose Paxos for the read optimization."

**Why this impresses:** You gave historical context, technical reasoning, and a nuanced conclusion. You didn't pick a side — you evaluated the tradeoff maturely.

events.title: Bigtable Paper Published
events.description: Spanner development begins as a successor to Bigtable. Initial goal: support globally consistent backups and schema changes.

events.title: Spanner Paper Released (OSDI)
events.description: Google publishes 'Spanner: Google's Globally-Distributed Database.' Introduces TrueTime, Paxos replication, and the directory abstraction to the world.

events.title: Spanner Becomes Google Cloud SQL
events.description: Google Cloud launches Cloud Spanner as a managed service. Initially SQL-based with limited features (no secondary indexes, no interleaved tables).

events.title: TrueTime Accuracy Improvement
events.description: Google publishes updated TrueTime data: 99th percentile clock uncertainty drops to ~7ms (from ~15ms in 2012). Intra-datacenter uncertainty < 1ms.

events.title: Spanner Becomes Multi-Cloud
events.description: Spanner supports multi-cloud deployment with cross-cloud replication. Customers can run Spanner across GCP + AWS for disaster recovery.

events.title: Spanner at 10B+ Queries/sec
events.description: Google reports Spanner handles 10B+ requests per second across all deployments. Schema change speed improves from hours to minutes with non-blocking DDL.

cards.title: TrueTime Bounds the Uncertainty
cards.body: TrueTime exposes time as an interval, not a point. The commit-wait mechanism (1-7ms) guarantees external consistency. Without hardware-backed clock sync, this is impossible at global scale.

cards.title: Paxos + 2PC for Transactions
cards.body: Single-group transactions use one Paxos round. Cross-group transactions use 2PC on top of Paxos. The directory abstraction minimizes cross-group transactions by co-locating related rows.

cards.title: Lock-Free Consistent Reads
cards.body: Read-only transactions pick a timestamp and execute at any sufficiently up-to-date replica. No locks, no blocking. Reads scale linearly with the number of replicas.

cards.title: Fresnel Reads
cards.body: Readers don't need locks because Spanner tracks the last-applied-write timestamp per replica. A reader waits (rarely) if the replica hasn't caught up. Average wait: < 1ms.

cards.title: External Consistency
cards.body: If transaction A finishes before transaction B starts, B will see A's writes. This is the strongest consistency model for distributed databases. No other global DB achieves it.

cards.title: Relational + SQL
cards.body: Unlike Bigtable (key-value) or Megastore (semi-relational), Spanner has schemas, SQL, and ACID transactions. SAP architects can map directly from HANA SQL to Spanner SQL.
